text: This is a video of a robotic toupee fundoplication by Dr. Crawford at the University of Illinois College of Medicine in Peoria. Toupee fundoplication refers to a posterior, two hundred and seventy degree fundal wrap. There are several different wraps which are commonly performed. Decisions regarding which fundoplication to perform is based on goals of surgery, surgeon preference, and patient factors. Fundoplication is used as part of the surgical treatment of various pathologies. It's also the primary surgical treatment of gastroesophageal reflux disease. Patients with GERD can be considered for anti-reflux surgery.

Absolute indications to treat GERD surgically are rare, so the risks and benefits of surgical intervention must be thoughtfully considered and operative candidates chosen selectively. The surgeon must identify the patient's symptoms, their impact on lifestyle, and response to medications. Mandatory diagnostic evaluation includes pH monitoring, upper endoscopy, manometry, and an esophagram. These studies can confirm the diagnosis, make alternative diagnoses, and define relevant anatomy, which all contribute to surgical decision making.

The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked, and a foot plate stabilizes them for steep reverse Trendelenburg positioning. Four trochars are placed across the upper abdomen, along with an air seal assistant port. A 5mm incision is made subxiphoid for the liver retractor. The surgeon mobilizes the greater curve from the gastric mid-body to the angle of His, dividing the gastro-splenic ligament with a vessel sealer device to prevent bleeding from short gastric vessels.

The surgeon divides the posterior fundal attachments to fully mobilize the fundus, separating attachments between the angle of His and the left crus. The plane between the crus and the esophagus is identified, and the gastrohepatic ligament is divided as the dissection progresses towards the right crus.

The surgeon identifies and develops the plane between the esophagus and the right cruse, carefully dissecting to avoid injury to the right vagus nerve, which is visible coursing from the right lateral side and deviating posteriorly along the esophagus. The phrenoesophageal membrane is divided, and further dissection clearly defines the anatomical structures of the stomach, esophagus, and cruse.

The surgeon identifies the left vagus nerve coursing anteriorly. Fat obscuring the lesser curve on the anterior stomach is removed to clarify the anatomy. An ethabond suture is placed at the gastroesophageal junction. Mediastinal areolar tissue is divided circumferentially to mobilize the esophagus, ensuring an adequate intraabdominal esophageal length of three to four centimeters.

The surgeon performs a cruroplasty, bringing the left and right crura together posteriorly with a plugeted ethobond suture to recreate the hiatus. The fundus is then brought posteriorly around the esophagus, with the left vagus nerve visible coursing anteriorly. The shoe shine maneuver is executed to symmetrically position the fundus.

Three sutures are placed to secure the fundus to the left side of the esophagus, ensuring the left vagus nerve is protected. The surgeon uses a sliding slip knot technique, starting with a square knot and adjusting the suture orientation by tugging the exiting leg. The knot is locked by pulling the suture ends in opposite directions. Subsequently, the right side of the fundoplication is secured to the esophagus with three interrupted ethabon sutures. Two clips are placed on the proximal sutures to mark the cephalad aspect of the wrap for future identification on x-ray images.

Postoperatively, patients are started on an antacid medication and a clear liquid diet. Carbonated beverages and straws, which can both lead to increased swallowed gas and cause bloating, are avoided. On post-op day one, they're advanced to full liquid diet and can be discharged. Patients stay on fulls for a week and are advanced to a soft diet for another week before returning to a general diet. Long-term outcomes are excellent with ninety percent of patients reporting improvement or resolution of heartburn.
